text: The surgeon makes a four-centimeter incision around the belly button to enter the abdominal cavity using a single incision technique.

The surgeon inserts a port into the right side of the abdomen, with visible ink marks indicating the location of the polyp. The cecum and small intestine are identified, and the surgical field shows minimal adhesions.
The surgeon reflects the transverse colon upwards, providing a clear view of the thin duodenum beneath.
The surgeon retracts the cecum to identify the vessel feeding the right colon, which is visibly suspended and tented up. The duodenum is also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon begins by opening the peritoneum beneath the vessel using an articulating end seal, creating a small window under the mesentery. The peritoneum is carefully separated to establish a space for further dissection.

The surgeon uses gentle sweeping motions to lift and separate the duodenum from the dissection area, creating more space. The duodenum is visible, along with small vessels descending with it, all situated in the retroperitoneal space. The peritoneum is being taken over the top of the duodenum.
The surgeon identifies a vessel with less surrounding tissue and prepares to ligate it, using standard technique.
The surgeon shows the ligated ileocolic artery with a visible pulsating stump. The duodenum is positioned safely below the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the tissue adjacent to the duodenum, continuing dissection in the retroperitoneal plane, ensuring that all retroperitoneal structures are brought down.

The surgeon uses a sweeping motion to dissect the retroperitoneal tissue, maintaining tension to facilitate the process. The colon becomes visible, indicating the dissection has reached the appropriate depth.
The surgeon removes non-essential fibers and proceeds to work on the lateral part of the surgical field, following successful medial mobilization of the colon.

The surgeon mobilizes the small bowel off the pelvic brim in the right lower quadrant, using tension applied by the left hand. The small intestine is visible, and the ureter runs underneath the area being dissected. The surgeon uses a cutting instrument to perform the dissection.

The surgeon crosses instruments to mobilize the right colon off the pelvic brim, revealing the undersurface of the retroperitoneum. Tension is applied to visualize the anatomical structures clearly during single-incision surgery.
